title: Reservoir-mediated spin entanglement in the mean-force Gibbs state
authors: L. A. Williamson, W. McEniery, F. Cerisola, J. Anders
abstract: Two qubits strongly coupled to a common bosonic reservoir can become entangled with each other, despite having no direct interaction. In equilibrium, such coupling-induced coherences can be described by the mean-force Gibbs state. Here we derive approximate, analytic expressions for the two-qubit mean-force Gibbs state, and use these to characterize equilibrium qubit-qubit entanglement mediated by a thermal reservoir. Entanglement, which is highest at lowest temperatures, is a non-monotonic function of the system-reservoir coupling strength. Moreover, we find that broadening the reservoir spectral density beyond a single mode, as is realistic for typical baths, can enhance the qubit entanglement. Our results provide a comprehensive understanding of reservoir-mediated two-qubit entanglement in thermal equilibrium and provide a benchmark to compare with numerical methods, as well as demonstrating the utility of strong system-reservoir coupling as a resource.
summary: This paper investigates how two qubits can become entangled through their shared interaction with a thermal bosonic reservoir. By using the mean-force Gibbs state, the authors show that entanglement is not simply a monotonic function of coupling strength but peaks at a specific value. They also demonstrate that a broader reservoir spectrum can actually enhance qubit entanglement compared to a single-mode reservoir. These findings provide a theoretical benchmark for studying strong-coupling effects in open quantum systems.
main_problem: Characterizing the nature of equilibrium entanglement between two qubits mediated by a common thermal bosonic reservoir under strong coupling.
main_result: The authors derive analytic expressions for the mean-force Gibbs state, finding that entanglement is a non-monotonic function of coupling strength and can be enhanced by broadening the reservoir spectral density.
method: The study employs the Mean-Force Gibbs (MFG) state framework, utilizing Reaction-Coordinate (RC) mapping, polaron transformations, and perturbative expansions in both coupling strength and spectral density width.
model_or_system: A spin-boson model consisting of two qubits (spin-1/2) strongly coupled to a common bosonic reservoir, characterized by a spectral density and reorganization energy.
key_observables: Negativity (as an entanglement monotone) and purity.
important_parameters: Coupling strength (lambda), temperature (T), reservoir spectral density width (gamma), qubit level spacing (omega_z), and qubit asymmetry (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to reach the mean-force Gibbs state; the perturbative expansion is valid to the second order in coupling strength; and the low-temperature limit is used for certain analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the system setup, RC mapping, and calculation regimes; Fig 2 shows negativity and purity as functions of spectral broadening; Fig 3 identifies the boundary between regimes where broadening enhances or decreases entanglement.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem and model, presents the derivation of the MFG state using RC mapping and perturbative methods, explores the effects of temperature and coupling strength, investigates the impact of spectral density broadening, and validates results via numerical diagonalization.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive understanding of how strong system-reservoir coupling and reservoir properties can be used as a resource to engineer entanglement in thermal equilibrium.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) System setup: two qubits (level spacings ωz(1 ± ϵ)) are coupled to a common bosonic reservoir with spectral density J(ω) and coupling strength λ. The mean- force Gibbs state is obtained by tracing out the bosonic reser- voir. (b) After the reaction-coordinate mapping, the qubits couple to a single bosonic mode (the reaction coordinate, fre- quency Ω) with coupling strength g ∝λ. The reaction co- ordinate couples to the remaining reservoir modes (spectral density JRC(ω)) with coupling strength λRC characterised by the width γ of J(ω). (c) Example reservoir spectral densities J(ω) (Eq. (16), solid line) and JRC(ω) (Eq. (17), dashed line), scaled by their maximum value, with γ...

FIG. 2. Thermal entanglement, quantified by the negativity N, for the mean-force Gibbs state of two qubits, which arises from the coupling to a common bosonic reservoir. (a,b) Entanglement mediated by a single-mode reservoir (γ →0) for varying T and g. Solid lines are obtained from ρnarrow MF [Eq. (14)], black dashed lines are obtained from numerical diagonalisation of ˆHRC S . (a) Entanglement decreases monotonically with increasing temperature and is absent for T ≥TN . Inset: purity of ρnarrow MF decreases with increasing temperature and, for kBT/Ω≲0.013, decreases with increasing g due to entanglement with the RC. (b) For fixed T ≤TN , entanglement of ρnarrow MF peaks at g = gpeak and...

FIG. 4. (a) Negativity as a function of qubit level spacing ωz with ϵ = 0. At T = 0 the entanglement increases mono- tonically with decreasing ωz/Ω. For a fixed T > 0, thermal fluctuations increase with decreasing ωz/Ω(which decreases ∆), resulting in a peak in entanglement. Diamonds mark the level spacing that gives ∆= kBT, at which point N is ap- proximately half its peak value. Vertical dotted line indicates ωz/Ω= 0.02. (b) Negativity as a function of asymmetry ϵ with ωz/Ω= 0.02, which is scaled by its ϵ = 0 value N0 for ease of comparison. At T = 0 the entanglement is indepen- dent of ϵ. For a fixed T > 0, thermal fluctuations increase with increasing ϵ, and hence N decreases. Solid...

FIG. 3. Lowest order correction to entanglement due to reservoir spectral density broadening at strong coupling, quantified via ∂N/∂γ2|γ=0 evaluated from Eq. (5). Entan- glement increases with γ (∂N/γ2 > 0) in the gray region and decreases with γ (∂N/∂γ2 < 0) in the red region. The bound- ary between these two regions follows gpeak (blue dashed line). Blue solid line indicates TN . Results are for ωz/Ω= 0.02 and ϵ = 0.
